questions.question_text: First aid in sports medicine is best defined as which one of the following types of care given to an injured athlete at the scene?

options.text: Definitive surgical management by a specialist doctor
Immediate temporary help given before professional medical care arrives
Long-term rehabilitation and physiotherapy programme
Routine annual medical examination of the athlete

questions.explanation: First aid is the immediate, temporary help given to an injured or suddenly ill person using available resources until qualified medical care takes over; its aims are to preserve life, prevent worsening of the condition and promote recovery.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Definitive surgery is beyond the scope of first aid.
Long-term rehabilitation follows first aid later.
Annual medical exams are screening, not first aid.

questions.question_text: Which of the following letters of the RICE protocol for acute soft-tissue injuries stands specifically for the elevation component of treatment?

options.text: R stands for elevation
I stands for elevation
C stands for elevation
E stands for elevation

questions.explanation: RICE stands for Rest, Ice, Compression and Elevation; the final 'E' is Elevation, where the injured part is raised above the level of the heart to reduce swelling by aiding venous and lymphatic drainage away from the area.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: R of RICE represents Rest of the injured part.
I of RICE stands for Ice application to tissue.
C of RICE stands for Compression bandaging.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate first-aid measure for a superficial abrasion on the knee of a young football player on the field?

options.text: Apply a tight tourniquet above the knee
Clean with saline or clean water and cover with sterile dressing
Cover with loose dirty cloth and ignore it
Rub with alcohol, ignore bleeding completely

questions.explanation: For a superficial abrasion, gently irrigate the wound with clean water or saline to remove debris, pat dry, apply a thin layer of antiseptic if available and cover with a sterile non-stick dressing; this prevents infection and supports healing.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Tourniquets are for severe arterial bleeding only.
Dirty cloth introduces infection risks to open wounds.
Rubbing alcohol on abrasions causes unnecessary tissue damage.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate duration and method of initial ice application in the RICE protocol for an acute ankle sprain?

options.text: Ice continuously for two hours directly on skin
Ice for 15–20 minutes wrapped in a thin cloth, repeated every 2 hours
Ice for five minutes only once every 24 hours
Hot water application for 30 minutes immediately

questions.explanation: Evidence-based RICE guidance recommends applying ice wrapped in a thin cloth for 15–20 minutes every 2–3 hours during the first 48 hours after injury; this controls swelling and pain without causing frostbite from direct skin contact.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Two continuous hours risks frostbite and tissue damage.
Five minutes a day is insufficient for acute injury control.
Heat worsens swelling in the first 48 hours post-injury.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate first-aid action for a cricketer experiencing muscle cramp in the calf during a long innings in hot weather?

options.text: Continue running at maximum speed ignoring the pain
Stop activity, gentle stretching, hydration with electrolytes
Apply hard firm pressure by striking the muscle repeatedly
Immerse the leg in ice water for over thirty minutes

questions.explanation: For muscle cramps, the athlete should stop activity, gently stretch the affected muscle, apply light massage, and rehydrate with water or an electrolyte drink; continuing to exert or using harsh mechanical force risks further muscle damage.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Running at maximum speed intensifies cramps sharply.
Striking the muscle can cause contusions and strains.
Prolonged ice immersion is not standard cramp care.

questions.question_text: Which of the following represents the most appropriate first-aid action for a player who has sustained a small blister on the sole of the foot during a long run?

options.text: Immediately puncture the blister with any sharp object
Keep the blister intact and cover with a soft padded dressing
Apply very hot water and continue running
Cut away the skin over the blister with scissors

questions.explanation: An intact blister roof provides a natural sterile cover; leaving it intact and protecting it with a soft padded dressing reduces infection risk and pain; if it must be drained, only a sterile needle at the edge should be used, keeping the skin roof in place.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Any sharp object introduces infection into the blister.
Hot water worsens damage and increases infection risk.
Cutting the skin exposes raw tissue to pathogens.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most appropriate first-aid technique for a suspected closed fracture of the lower arm sustained in a fall during a skating event?

options.text: Attempt to realign the bone aggressively on site
Immobilise the arm with a padded splint and transport to hospital
Apply vigorous deep tissue massage to the fracture site
Allow the athlete to continue skating with minor discomfort

questions.explanation: Suspected closed fractures should be immobilised in the position found using a padded splint that spans the joints above and below the break, then the athlete should be safely transported for imaging; any attempt to reduce on site can worsen injury.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Aggressive realignment can pierce blood vessels or nerves.
Massage is contraindicated over a fracture site.
Continued activity can displace the fracture further.

questions.question_text: Which of the following best describes the correct chest compression rate and depth recommended for adult CPR delivered on a collapsed athlete with no pulse?

options.text: 30 compressions per minute at 1 cm depth
100–120 compressions per minute at 5–6 cm depth
200 compressions per minute at 10 cm depth
50 compressions per minute at 2 cm depth

questions.explanation: Current adult CPR guidelines recommend 100–120 compressions per minute at a depth of 5–6 cm over the centre of the chest, with full chest recoil between compressions and minimal interruptions; 30 compressions to 2 breaths is the standard ratio.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: 30 per minute at 1 cm is far below effective CPR standards.
200 per minute at 10 cm exceeds safe recommendations.
50 per minute at 2 cm is too slow and too shallow.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the correct ratio of chest compressions to rescue breaths used by a single lay rescuer during adult cardiopulmonary resuscitation on a collapsed player?

options.text: 5 compressions to 1 breath
15 compressions to 2 breaths
30 compressions to 2 breaths
50 compressions to 5 breaths

questions.explanation: For a single adult lay rescuer, the recommended CPR ratio is 30 chest compressions to 2 rescue breaths, continued in cycles until advanced life-support arrives or the victim recovers; the ratio aims to balance circulation with ventilation.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: 5:1 is outdated paediatric two-rescuer practice.
15:2 is used for two-rescuer paediatric CPR.
50:5 is not a recognised CPR ratio in any setting.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the correct first-aid approach for a swimmer rescued from near-drowning who is breathing spontaneously but remains unconscious on the pool deck?

options.text: Place in recovery position and monitor airway closely
Force large amounts of water orally to rinse the stomach
Sit the person upright tightly in a chair and strap them down
Allow the person to lie flat on back with mouth closed forcibly

questions.explanation: For an unconscious but breathing near-drowning victim, the recovery position keeps the airway open and allows any aspirated water or vomit to drain; close monitoring of breathing and pulse continues until emergency medical services take over.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Forcing oral fluids risks aspiration pneumonia.
Strapping the victim upright compromises airway.
Lying flat with mouth forced shut blocks airway.

questions.question_text: Match each first-aid scenario in Column A with the most appropriate immediate action listed in Column B and choose the correct mapping option below.

column_b.text: Gently stretch the muscle and rehydrate
Clean and cover with sterile dressing

questions.explanation: Acute sprains respond best to RICE; superficial cuts require cleaning and sterile covering; cramps need gentle stretching plus rehydration; an unconscious but breathing victim is placed in the recovery position to maintain an open airway.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Acute sprains must receive the RICE protocol first.
Small bleeding cuts need cleaning and covering.
Recovery position is specifically for unconscious breathing victims.

questions.question_text: Match each letter of the RICE protocol in Column A with its corresponding explanation in Column B and choose the correct combination from the options below.

column_b.text: Elevate above heart to reduce swelling
Stop activity and protect the part
Apply ice wrapped in thin cloth

questions.explanation: R — Rest and protect the injured part; I — Ice wrapped in thin cloth applied intermittently; C — Compression with an elastic bandage to limit swelling; E — Elevation above heart level to aid venous and lymphatic drainage.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Rest is the first step to prevent further damage.
Compression reduces swelling mechanically.
Elevation works by aiding venous drainage.

questions.question_text: Match each type of sports injury in Column A with the most specific first-aid item required from Column B and choose the correct combination from the options.

column_a.text: Severe anaphylactic reaction to a bee sting

questions.explanation: A bleeding wound needs sterile gauze with pressure dressing; a long-bone fracture demands a padded splint and sling; acute contusions are treated with ice; anaphylaxis requires immediate intramuscular adrenaline auto-injector administration.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Bleeding requires pressure dressing as priority.
Fractures always require splinting and support.
Anaphylaxis demands immediate adrenaline injection.

questions.question_text: Read the assertion and reason about the RICE protocol carefully and choose the option that best describes their logical relationship in acute injury care.
questions.assertion: RICE protocol is the standard first-aid treatment for most acute soft-tissue injuries in the first 48 hours after the event.
questions.reason: Rest, ice, compression and elevation together reduce bleeding, swelling and pain in the acute injury phase of tissue damage.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: Rest prevents further damage; ice vasoconstricts to reduce bleeding and pain; compression limits swelling mechanically; elevation aids venous drainage; together they directly address the key acute-phase pathology explaining RICE's widespread use.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: The reason accurately describes the pathophysiology.
Both statements are widely supported in sports medicine.
RICE directly targets acute-phase tissue response.

questions.question_text: Examine the assertion and reason regarding early versus late first aid in sports and choose the option that best represents their logical connection on the field.
questions.assertion: Prompt on-field first aid significantly improves the long-term outcome of most sports injuries compared to delayed care.
questions.reason: Early first aid limits secondary tissue damage from bleeding, swelling and oxygen deprivation in injured tissues.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: After the primary injury, uncontrolled bleeding, swelling and hypoxia cause secondary cell death in adjacent tissues; prompt first aid with pressure, ice and immobilisation halts this cascade, directly improving long-term recovery outcomes.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Secondary damage is a well-recognised pathological process.
Both statements are grounded in clinical evidence.
The reason correctly explains the assertion mechanism.

questions.question_text: Consider the assertion and reason concerning heat applied to acute sports injuries and pick the option that correctly describes their logical relationship.
questions.assertion: Heat should not be applied to an acute sports injury within the first 48 hours after the event.
questions.reason: Heat causes vasodilation that increases bleeding and swelling in freshly damaged tissue during the acute phase.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are true; Reason correctly explains Assertion
Both are true but Reason does not correctly explain Assertion
Assertion is true but Reason is false
Assertion is false but Reason is true

questions.explanation: In the first 48 hours, damaged vessels are still bleeding and inflammation is rising; heat causes vasodilation and amplifies these changes, worsening swelling and pain, so cold therapy is preferred until the acute phase settles.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Heat is contraindicated in the acute post-injury phase.
The reason correctly describes vasodilation effects.
Both statements reflect standard sports-medicine guidance.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following steps of the RICE protocol as they should be applied in correct chronological order to an acute ankle sprain on the field.

items.text: Apply compression with an elastic bandage
Rest and remove from play immediately
Elevate the limb above heart level
Apply ice wrapped in thin cloth

options.text: Q → S → P → R
R → P → S → Q
S → Q → R → P
P → R → Q → S

questions.explanation: The standard RICE sequence is: first Rest and remove from play, then apply Ice wrapped to avoid frostbite, then add Compression with an elastic bandage, and finally Elevate the limb above heart level to aid drainage.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Rest is always the first step in acute injury.
Ice is applied before compression in RICE.
Elevation is the final component of RICE.

questions.question_text: Arrange the following steps of the DRSABCD primary survey for a collapsed athlete on the sports field into the correct chronological order used in first aid.

items.text: Check response — speak and gently shake
Check danger — make scene safe first
Send for help — call emergency services

options.text: Q → P → R → S
P → Q → S → R
R → S → P → Q
S → R → P → Q

questions.explanation: The DRSABCD primary survey moves from Danger (make scene safe), to Response (check consciousness), to Send for help, to Airway and Breathing, followed by CPR and Defibrillation if needed — a structured approach that prioritises safety.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Scene safety must be the first step always.
Response check precedes calling for help.
Airway and breathing follow after help is summoned.

questions.question_text: State whether the following statement is true or false: Applying ice directly onto bare skin for long durations during RICE is considered safe and recommended practice.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Ice should always be wrapped in a thin damp cloth before skin application; direct prolonged contact can cause ice burn or frostbite of the superficial tissues, which adds injury instead of treating the original sports trauma.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Direct ice on skin causes frostbite risks.
A thin cloth barrier is always recommended.
Long durations of direct ice are contraindicated.

questions.question_text: Study the diagram of the RICE protocol and identify which component primarily works by vasoconstriction to reduce bleeding and pain in acute soft-tissue injuries.

options.text: Rest
Ice
Compression
Elevation

questions.explanation: Ice causes local vasoconstriction and slows nerve conduction, thereby reducing bleeding into the injured tissue, limiting swelling and decreasing pain; Rest prevents further injury, Compression mechanically limits oedema, and Elevation uses gravity to drain fluid.
questions.subject: Physical Education

elimination_hints.hint: Rest prevents further damage but does not vasoconstrict.
Compression works mechanically, not thermally.
Elevation works via gravity, not vasoconstriction.
